title: Mortgage Life Insurance in Canada
seo_title: Mortgage Life Insurance Canada | Is It Worth It? | PolicyScanner
seo_description: Compare mortgage life insurance vs. term life insurance in Canada. Find out what banks don't tell you and get better coverage for less. Ontario-licensed advisors.

secondary_keywords: bank mortgage insurance vs term life
_meta.conversion_goal: Lead capture — convince homeowners to choose term life over bank mortgage insurance, then get quotes through PolicyScanner
_meta.page_role: Product page targeting homeowners. Educates on bank CI/mortgage insurance weakness vs. independent term life. High-intent audience: recent homebuyers.
_meta.writer_focus: This page has a clear editorial position: bank mortgage insurance (creditor insurance) is almost always worse than owning a personal term life policy. That's the honest, defensible truth. Write it clearly without being inflammatory.

seo_keywords.density_rule: Use 'mortgage life insurance' 2–3 times per 500 words. Also use 'mortgage protection' and 'creditor insurance' naturally.
natural_variations: if you die with a mortgage
covering the balance on your home loan
seo_keywords.real_example_to_use: Mark and Lisa, Brampton — bought $850K home, declined bank mortgage insurance, got $1M 20-year term policy for $78/month instead of $210/month — and the payout goes to their family, not the bank
seo_keywords.avoid: Do not say bank mortgage insurance is a scam — say it's a weaker product and explain why. Factual and defensible.

page_data.heading: Mortgage Life Insurance: What the Bank Sells vs. What You Should Buy
page_data.subheading: Your bank will offer you mortgage insurance at closing. Here's what they don't tell you — and why most Canadians choose personal term life instead.

_writer_notes.focus: Provocative but fair. Acknowledge bank mortgage insurance exists, then frame the comparison. Don't dismiss — educate.
key_points: Bank mortgage insurance protects the bank. Personal term life protects your family.
Most homeowners can get better coverage for the same — or less — money

page_data.heading: The Problem with Bank Mortgage Insurance

_writer_notes.focus: Explain the 3 core weaknesses of bank creditor insurance: post-claim underwriting, decreasing benefit, bank as beneficiary.
key_points: Post-claim underwriting: bank approves you at sale, but reviews your health record AFTER you die. If they find undisclosed health issues, claim denied.
Decreasing benefit: your mortgage balance decreases over time. Your premiums don't. You pay the same but your coverage shrinks.
Bank is the beneficiary: money goes directly to the bank to pay off the mortgage — your family doesn't receive cash to use as they need
Not portable: if you switch lenders or refinance, you lose coverage
Group underwritten: everyone in the same age band pays the same regardless of health — healthy people subsidize unhealthy ones
_writer_notes.structure: Use 5 clearly labelled problems. Numbered list with bold problem name + 2-sentence explanation each.

page_data.heading: Bank Mortgage Insurance vs. Personal Term Life Insurance

_writer_notes.focus: Side-by-side comparison table. Be objective but let the facts speak.
key_points: Underwriting: post-claim (bank) vs. pre-approved (term life)
Benefit: decreasing balance (bank) vs. level death benefit (term)
Beneficiary: bank (bank) vs. your family (term)
Portability: no (bank) vs. yes (term — follows you wherever you go)
Price: often higher per dollar of coverage (bank) vs. usually lower (term)
Flexibility: pays mortgage only (bank) vs. family uses money however needed (term)
_writer_notes.real_example: Mark and Lisa example: $850K mortgage → bank offered insurance at $210/month for $850K decreasing coverage. PolicyScanner found $1M 20-year term for $78/month — more coverage, lower cost, family as beneficiary.

page_data.heading: When Does Bank Mortgage Insurance Make Sense?

_writer_notes.focus: Be fair — bank mortgage insurance isn't always the wrong choice. Cover edge cases.
key_points: If you have an uninsurable health condition and can't get personal coverage
Short-term bridge: if you need coverage immediately while a personal policy is being underwritten
Convenience: some buyers just want to check the box at closing — bank makes it easy
_writer_notes.structure: Short section. 3 scenarios. Then: 'For most healthy Canadians under 60, however, a personal term life policy is almost always better value.'

page_data.heading: Cost Comparison: What You'd Actually Pay

_writer_notes.focus: Real numbers. $600K mortgage example. Bank insurance vs. $600K 20-year term life.
key_points: Bank mortgage insurance (Big 5 bank): ~$130–$180/month for $600K
$600K 20-year term life (personal), age 35, non-smoker, male: ~$60–$80/month
$600K 20-year term life (personal), age 35, non-smoker, female: ~$45–$65/month
Personal term: more coverage, lower cost, family is beneficiary
Rate increases: bank insurance premiums increase at each renewal; level-term stays flat for 20 years
_writer_notes.structure: Table comparing monthly cost at different mortgage sizes ($400K, $600K, $800K). Source: illustrative estimates — recommend they get a real quote.

page_data.heading: How to Replace Bank Mortgage Insurance with a Personal Policy

_writer_notes.focus: Action steps for someone who wants to switch. Simple 4-step process.
key_points: Step 1: Get a personal term life quote — PolicyScanner compares 10+ insurers
Step 2: Apply and get approved (medical questionnaire, health check if needed)
Step 3: Once approved and policy active, cancel bank mortgage insurance
IMPORTANT: Do not cancel bank coverage before personal policy is active
Step 4: Update beneficiary to spouse / estate — not the bank

_writer_notes.focus: FSRA regulates the insurance products. Banks sell insurance under special federal bank charters — different oversight than FSRA-licensed brokers. This matters for accountability.
key_points: Bank-sold creditor insurance is federally regulated under OSFI — different rules than FSRA
Personal term life sold by licensed brokers = FSRA regulated with fiduciary obligation
FCAC (Financial Consumer Agency of Canada) has issued consumer alerts about creditor insurance weaknesses
Ontario advisors at PolicyScanner are FSRA-licensed and legally required to act in your best interest

page_data.heading: Mortgage Life Insurance — Frequently Asked Questions

faqs.question: Is mortgage life insurance mandatory in Canada?
faqs.answer: No. Mortgage life insurance is optional in Canada. Lenders cannot require you to buy their mortgage insurance product. You can satisfy their protection requirement with a personal life insurance policy instead.
faqs._guidance: Common misconception — banks sometimes imply it's required. Be clear it's not.

faqs.question: What is post-claim underwriting?
faqs.answer: Post-claim underwriting means the insurer waits until a claim is filed to fully review your health history. You were approved at the point of sale, but their investigation happens after your death — meaning claims can be denied after the fact. Personal life insurance policies use pre-claim underwriting: your health is fully reviewed before approval.
faqs._guidance: This is the most important concept on the page. Explain clearly.

faqs.question: Can I get mortgage insurance if I have health issues?
faqs.answer: Bank creditor insurance is often easier to qualify for because it uses simplified (group) underwriting. However, the trade-off is weaker claims coverage and higher cost. If you have a serious health history, a no-medical or simplified-issue term life policy may be worth exploring as an alternative.
faqs._guidance: Fair answer — acknowledge the accessibility of bank insurance for harder-to-insure individuals.

faqs.question: What happens to mortgage insurance if I sell my home?
faqs.answer: Bank mortgage insurance is tied to your specific mortgage at that lender. If you sell or refinance, the coverage ends. Personal term life insurance is not tied to your mortgage — it follows you, stays in force, and protects your family regardless of where you live.
faqs._guidance: Portability is a key differentiator — reinforce it here.

faqs.question: What happens if both spouses die at the same time?
faqs.answer: With bank mortgage insurance, the mortgage is paid off and nothing goes to heirs. With personal term life insurance, each policy pays its full benefit to the named beneficiary — leaving your family with a cash benefit they can use for anything, not just the mortgage.
faqs._guidance: This is an important scenario for couples — explain clearly.
